title: Alvirto Smith v Shalia Gardiner (CL APM 03/23) [2026] TCASC 17 (11 March 2026)
text:
IN THE SUPREME COURT OF THE ACTION NO. CL APM 03/23
TURKS AND CAICOS ISLANDS
BETWEEN:
ALVIRTO ALI ALEXANDER SMITH APPELLANT
-and-
SHALIA GARDINER RESPONDENT
Before: The Hon. Mr Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Appearances: Mr Smith pro se
Ms Gardiner pro se
Hearing Date: 14th July 2025
Venue: Supreme Court Annexe, Leeward Highway, Providenciales.
JUDGMENT
1. This is an appeal from the decision of HH Keri-ann Kemble, Resident Magistrate, given
on 30 October 2023, where she ordered Mr Smith to pay Ms Gardiner the sum of
US$7,000.00 by way of refund for monies paid to Mr Smith on 11 October 2022, for the
purchase of a 2008 Nissan Dualis SUV motor vehicle.
2. The learned Magistrate held:
“It stood out to the Court that the contract was never completed.
It is a logical condition of the contract for sale of a vehicle that legal ownership
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 1 of 16

be transferred to the purchaser to afford the purchaser the full benefit of
ownership i.e. the ability to sell and transfer legal ownership of the vehicle to
another. Indeed, the purchaser having paid in full for the vehicle could sue to
have the seller transfer legal ownership. In this case to date, the vehicle is still
in the name of the Defendant…
Instead of arrangements being made to complete the contract by attending [at]
Road Traffic and doing the necessaries – the Defendant proceeded instead to
ignore the Plaintiff over a period of time…
This failure on the Defendant’s part to fully transfer the vehicle to the Plaintiff
is a breach of contract.”
The Evidence
3. In or around September 2022, the Appellant, Mr Smith, advertised a 2008 Nissan Dualis
motor vehicle for sale at an asking price of US$8,800.00. The advertisement stated that
the vehicle was to be sold ‘as is’.
4. The Respondent, Ms Gardiner, knew Mr Smith and contacted him with a view to her
purchasing the vehicle. Ms Gardiner went to see the vehicle and was advised by Mr Smith
that the vehicle could not be started, but that he was going to take it to his mechanic and
have some work done on the vehicle. A price of US$7,500.00 was agreed. Mr Smith
advised that he was going to get the vehicle serviced.
5. On 11th October 2022, the car was ready and Mr Smith took it to Ms Gardiner’s home. Ms
Gardiner took the vehicle for a test drive and was concerned that the engine maintenance
lights were illuminated. She says that Mr Smith said that they would turn themselves off
after she had driven for a while. Mr Smith says that the lights were on because the car had
needed a service. That is why he had taken it to his mechanic, who had not reset the
warning lights. He said that there was a sequence which could be followed to reset the
lights, and he told Ms Gardiner that there was a video on ‘YouTube’ which she could
follow. Ms Gardiner also says that whilst on the test drive, she started hearing a loud sound
and asked Mr Smith what the noise was. She says that Mr Smith said to her that she would
hear that from time to time as a result of the work carried out by his mechanic. These
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 2 of 16

events did not appear to alert Ms Gardiner that there were issues with the vehicle, nor that
she perhaps should have it looked over by a mechanic of her choosing.
6. It is common ground that Ms Gardiner was US$500.00 short when the vehicle was
delivered to her, and Mr Smith agreed to accept the US$7,000.00 as full payment, and he
gave Ms Gardiner a receipt.
7. Ms Gardiner says that Mr Smith said he would draw up a contract and/or bill of sale the
next day. Mr Smith said that was not the case and that they both had to attend the
Department of Motor Vehicles (‘DMV’) to have the vehicle licensed, registered in her
name and to transfer title. Mr Smith agrees that he told Ms Gardiner that the vehicle
insurance and registration were up-to-date, so that she could drive the car without issue
until they could attend at the DMV. He also confirmed that he agreed that he would go to
DMV with Ms Gardiner in order to transfer the registration, but this did not occur.
8. Ms Gardiner complains that she did not receive any paperwork for the vehicle, no
registration, title or insurance documentation.
9. The next day, Ms Gardiner was using the vehicle, and it shut off. This happened on more
than 1 occasion and she got her mechanic to take a look. There is some dispute over the
work that was carried out by Mr Smith’s mechanic and that which Ms Gardiner’s
mechanic thought was required, but that is not something which the Court has to decide.
10. Ms Gardiner says that she tried to contact Mr Smith by messaging him, but that he didn’t
respond. In the end, she decided to take the vehicle to a reputable garage, having described
her mechanic as a ‘bush mechanic’.
11. Ms Gardiner put into evidence an inspection report dated 25 October 2022 from D and B
Auto Repair, which listed a significant number of parts that the vehicle needed replacing,
including front and rear sub-frames, engine mounts, new axles, shock absorbers, etc. No
cost estimate was provided. The inspection report also stated that the rear brake lines were
leaking and that the vehicle was not safe to drive. No challenge has been made to the
report and findings. I therefore accept that it records an accurate state of condition.
12. Ms Gardiner then says she tried to contact Mr Smith on a number of occasions, but she
was unable to get any response. As a result, on 28 October 2022, she took the vehicle to
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 3 of 16

Mr Smith’s property and parked it in his yard, and requested her money back by text
message, in the belief that she thought she had the right to do so.
13. A Mr Kendrick Gardiner Jr gave evidence at the trial before the learned Magistrate. He is
Mr Smith’s cousin and was house-sitting for Mr Smith when the vehicle was returned. He
stated that he noticed the vehicle had been left at Mr Smith’s property on 26 October 2022,
at which time he says he contacted the police, but they did not take any action. He says
around a month later, he called the police again and he was advised to get a tow truck to
have the vehicle removed. Accordingly, he called a tow truck who removed the vehicle
on or about 30 November 2022.
14. Mr Smith in his evidence before me said that he contacted his cousin and told him to
contact the police and then have the vehicle towed. He said the vehicle had been there for
6 weeks, but that he made no contact with Ms Gardiner as she had indicated that she was
going to bring this case. Mr Smith also says that he did not know what happened to the
vehicle after it had been towed and that he did not pay the towing company to remove the
vehicle.
15. Ms Gardiner now says, although it was not mentioned in the learned Magistrate’s notes
of evidence, that she has contacted the towing company and has been told that the vehicle
has been broken up and the parts sold off.
16. The vehicle is now a total loss.
The Appeal
17. I set out my opinion as to how an appeal from the Magistrate is properly put before the
Court in Fritz Jean-Francois -v- Leonorah Missick1. This appeal was commenced
before that judgment was handed down and so has been brought as I set out in paragraph
16 of that judgment. The learned Magistrate delivered her decision on 30 November 2023.
A notice of appeal was filed in the Magistrate’s Court, and Mr Smith entered into a
recognisance to prosecute the appeal on the same day. The notice of appeal was served on
Ms Gardiner on 3 November 2023, within the 5 days allowed.
1 [2024] TCASC 18; CL APM 1/23.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 4 of 16

18. An appeal from the Magistrate to the Supreme Court is by way of rehearing2. The appeal
is heard on the record of evidence considered by the previous decision maker and the Court
has the power to receive further evidence on questions of fact.3
19. I took further evidence from both Ms Gardiner and Mr Smith.
Discussion
20. Whilst Ms Gardiner provided extensive evidence regarding the vehicle's condition, its
issues with breaking down, and alleged representations made by Mr Smith, which the
Court is asked to infer as misrepresentations, this claim is not based on those grounds. The
learned Magistrate also did not rule in favour of Ms Gardiner due to breach of
representation, but on the finding that Mr Smith failed to transfer legal title.
21. Absent any expressed representation as to quality, then the maxim ‘caveat emptor’4
applies. Section 16 of the Sale of Goods Act (Cap. 17.10) (‘SGA’) provides:
Implied terms about quality or fitness
16. (1) Except as provided by this section and section 17,5 and subject to any
other enactment, there is no implied condition or warranty about the quality or
fitness for any particular purpose of goods supplied under a contract of sale.
22. Implied terms as to quality and fitness for purposes are only implied where the goods are
sold in the course of a business.6 There is no suggestion that Mr Smith was acting in the
course of a business, and this was a private sale.
23. Ms Gardiner stated that she did not carry out any checks on the roadworthiness of the
vehicle, and in response to a question from the Court, she conceded that Mr Smith did not
say that the vehicle was in good order, but that he was taking the car to his mechanic to
make sure it was ok. Ms Gardiner confirmed that she did not speak to Mr Smith’s
mechanic. Ms Gardiner then backtracked and said that Mr Smith said he was making sure
that the vehicle was ‘in good working order’. Ms Gardiner also confirmed that Mr Smith
2 Supreme Court Practice 1999 – O.55 – Note 55/0/2
3 Supreme Court Rules 2000 - O.55 r.7.
4 Let the buyer beware. In short, a private buyer buys the goods at his/her own risk.
5 Sale by sample.
6 Sub-sections 16(2) and (3) SGA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 5 of 16

did not tell her that any of the issues found by the D and B Auto Repair later inspection
were in good order.7
24. Ms Gardiner stated that she did not get any paperwork for the vehicle other than a receipt
for the monies paid. She said that Mr Smith said he would give her a contract and bill of
sale, but these never materialised, and as a result, she asserts that legal title to the vehicle
was never transferred into her name. Ms Gardiner also says that she never received any
title document or registration document for the vehicle. It is on this basis that Ms Gardiner
says the contract was never concluded, with which the learned Magistrate agreed.
25. The four elements for the formation of a contract are trite law. There must be:
a. An offer. In this case an offer to purchase the vehicle at a cost of US$7,000.00 was
made.
b. Acceptance. Mr Smith accepted Ms Gardiner’s offer8 to buy the vehicle at a price
of US$7,000.00.
c. Consideration. Mr Smith accepted the payment of US$7,000.00, gave a receipt and
delivered up the vehicle to Ms Gardiner. There was, I find, good consideration by
both parties, i.e. the payment of money and the delivery up of the vehicle.
d. An intention to create a legal relationship. This is not in dispute. Mr Smith intended
to sell the vehicle to Ms Gardiner, and Ms Gardiner intended to buy it.
26. The learned Magistrate held:
“It was not in issue that there was a contract between the parties concerning the
Nissan.”
27. In ordinary circumstances, under the common law, absent any additional terms of
agreement, the above steps would have concluded the contract, i.e. title would have passed
to Ms Gardiner upon the irrevocable agreement to buy and sell the vehicle, or at the latest,
upon delivery to Ms Gardiner.
7 See para. 11 above.
8 The vehicle had been advertised for sale at US$8,000.00. The Respondent offered US7,500.00, but when it came
to payment, only had US$7,000.00. The Appellant agreed to accept the US$7,000.00 in full payment.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 6 of 16

28. The issues for the Court, as asserted by Ms Gardiner, are whether promises were made by
Mr Smith to give to Ms Gardiner:
a. a written contract and a bill of sale;
b. the registration document for the vehicle;
c. a title document; and
d. insurance documents.
were terms of the contract for the sale of the vehicle, and if so, did not producing these
documents mean that the legal title to the vehicle had not been vested in Ms Gardiner, as
the learned Magistrate found.
29. Mr Smith, in his evidence, stated that he was of the understanding that they had to go to
DMV in order to complete a ‘Bill of Sale’ to transfer title and register the vehicle in Ms
Gardiner’s name.
The Road Traffic Act (Cap. 13.01)
30. The Road Traffic Act (‘RTA’) and the regulations made thereunder have certain
provisions dealing with the registration and licensing of motor vehicles.
31. Section 5 RTA provides inter alia:
Licensing and registration of motor vehicles
(1) Every motor vehicle or trailer imported into the Islands before being used
otherwise on the road shall be taken from the point of importation direct to an
inspector appointed under section 3(2)(a) (hereinafter referred to as a “vehicle
inspector”), or direct to a warehouse, for examination, and no such vehicle shall
be used further on the road otherwise than by a vehicle inspector for testing
purposes until a vehicle inspector has certified the vehicle fit for use on the road
and has issued in respect of the vehicle a prescribed certificate of
roadworthiness.
(2) Any person who uses or permits to be used on a road any motor vehicle or
trailer for which any licence under this Part has not been issued or has expired
commits an offence.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 7 of 16

(5) The Director, on being satisfied that the provisions of the Motor Vehicles
(Third Party Insurance) Ordinance have been complied with shall, on receipt of
payment of the prescribed fee, register the motor vehicle by assigning it a serial
registration number, recording the prescribed particulars in the register, and shall
issue to the owner an identification plate and a licence in the prescribed form.
… (Emphasis added)
32. Section 9 RTA provides:
(5) Where a registered vehicle is sold or otherwise transferred the transferor shall
so inform the Director in the prescribed form within seven days after the transfer
and shall remove and retain the identification plate of the vehicle, and the
transferee shall submit to the Director the application for registration and the
vehicle licence. (Amended by Ords. 1 of 2003 and 13 of 2019) (Emphasis added)
(6) The Director shall consider the application for registration of the vehicle as
if it were an application, mutatis mutandis, under section 5, but shall not issue
an identification plate in respect of the vehicle where the transferee has already
been issued an identification plate which is not assigned to a registered vehicle,
in which case the transferee shall affix that identification plate to the vehicle in
the prescribed manner, and the transferor of the vehicle shall, upon becoming
the registered owner of another vehicle, affix the identification plate to that
vehicle in the prescribed manner.
33. Section 62 RTA provides:
Regulations
62. (1) The Governor may make regulations prescribing any matter which by
this Ordinance may be prescribed and concerning any matter which appears to
him to be necessary or desirable for giving effect to this Ordinance.
Road Traffic Regulations (‘the Regulations’)
34. Regulation 46 provides:
Transfer of registration and fee
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 8 of 16

46. (1) Subject to section 9(4) of the Ordinance, on the change of ownership of
a motor vehicle, the registered owner and the new owner shall sign the form set
out in Schedule 8 and the transferee shall submit the form to the Director together
with the fee of $20. (Emphasis added)
35. Regulation 46 is expressly subject to section 9(4) of the Act. Section 9(4) deals with the
offences concerning identification plates. It is section 9(5) which deals with the sale or
other transfer of a registered vehicle. It appears that the reference to section 9(4) of the
RTA is an error.
36. Regulation 47 provides:
Transfer of ownership
47. On the transfer of ownership of a motor vehicle, the Director shall amend the
register, the registration and the licence. (Inserted by L.N. 40/2019)
37. The form set out in Schedule 8 (‘the Prescribed Form’) is titled ‘Transfer of Motor
Vehicle’, albeit I take judicial notice that this is colloquially known as a ‘Bill of Sale’.9 It
provides inter alia for details to be given of the seller, the buyer, the price paid and details
of the vehicle and current registration number. It is to be signed by both the seller and the
buyer, and a witness. As noted in paragraph 34 above, it is for the transferee (or buyer) to
submit the form.
38. There is therefore an apparent tension between the Act and the Regulations. Under the
Act, it is for the seller to inform the Director10 of the sale in the Prescribed Form within 7
days after the transfer. Under the Regulations, the transferee (or the buyer) is to submit
the Prescribed Form.
39. The Act also provides that the purchaser must submit an application for registration and
for a vehicle licence. In doing so, section 5 RTA is triggered, as if the vehicle has just
been imported. When the registration/licensing process is completed, the vehicle is
registered and licensed to the purchaser.
9 As referred to by Mr Smith – see para. 29 above.
10 The Director of Road Safety and Transportation – See section 2 of the RTA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 9 of 16

40. I was not addressed as to whether the submission of a completed Prescribed Form by the
buyer is accepted by the Director as informing him of the sale or other transfer of a vehicle.
If it is not accepted, then as Mr Smith appears to believe, it would seem that both the seller
and the buyer need to attend at the DMV. Whether they need to attend at the same time is
unclear.
41. The above process does not require the seller to deliver to the purchaser the seller’s
registration form for the vehicle. Ms Gardiner would get a new registration in her name.
Likewise, a seller is not in my view, under any obligation to deliver up their insurance
documents.
42. The learned Magistrate based her decision on the fact that Mr Smith never attended at the
DMV and as such, did not complete the contract. She must therefore have considered that
Mr Smith:
a. was obligated to attend as a contractual obligation; and
b. that he declined or otherwise neglected to do so;
However, the learned Magistrate recorded:
“The defendant testified in examination in chief:
‘Plans were made Ms Gardiner and I to attend the Road Traffic Department to
have the transfer documents and contract completed. However, this never
happened. We were to go to Road Safety anytime Ms Gardiner was ready to go
to Road Safety but she and I would have to go there. No date had been agreed
at that time. I never spoke to Ms Gardiner after the day I give her the receipt
about having the contract completed.’”
43. Ms Gardiner, in her evidence before me, said that there was no firm arrangement to attend
at the DMV and that when she found there were issues with the vehicle, she returned it to
Mr Smith, by parking it in his yard and then messaged him, asking for her money back.
44. There are, therefore, 2 different issues. The learned Magistrate found that there had been
a breach of contract by non-attendance at the DMV, but Ms Gardiner was of the view that
she could reject the vehicle due to the faults and demand her money back, and as such
never asked Mr Gardiner to attend at the DMV.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 10 of 16

45. I am of the view that if there is a requirement for both parties to attend at the DMV and
Mr Smith refused or neglected to do so, then the learned Magistrate was correct to find a
breach of contract, although not one which affected the legal title, to which I return below.
I come to that conclusion on the basis that if the local conditions require attendance, then
such attendance would be an implied term of the contract; however, I cannot, on the
evidence, find on the balance of probabilities that Mr Smith neglected or refused as events
took over and Ms Gardiner sought to reject the vehicle.
46. I am not of the view that the process required by the RTA and by the DMV has any effect
on legal title to the vehicle. Legal title to a motor vehicle is, in my view, no different to
legal title to any other property and title transfers when the parties intend it to transfer.
Section 19 SGA provides:
Property passes when intended to pass
19. (1) Where there is a contract for the sale of specific or ascertained goods the
property in them is transferred to the buyer at such time as the parties to the
contract intend it to be transferred.
(2) For the purposes of ascertaining the intention11 of the parties, regard shall be
had to the terms of the contract, the conduct of the parties and the circumstances
of the case.
47. The purpose and process required by the RTA of registration and licensing of a vehicle
after a sale within the Islands is to record the transfer of title of a vehicle (or ownership)
and to allow the use of the vehicle on public roads. The form set out in Schedule 8 of the
Road Traffic Regulations is to inform the Director that a transfer of ownership has taken
place so that he may discharge his duty under Regulation 47, which provides:
Transfer of ownership
47. On the transfer of ownership of a motor vehicle, the Director shall amend the
register, the registration and the licence.
48. In my view, subsequent registration and licensing after a vehicle has been sold is not
11 Rules for ascertaining intention are set out in section 20 SGA.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 11 of 16

required to transfer the legal title; the emphasised extracts from the RTA and the
Regulations anticipate that legal title has already passed. That said, I can see
administrative issues following a subsequent transfer and attempt to register and licence,
if a former transfer has not been registered.
49. That does not dispose of the matter. I have set out in paragraphs 20 to 22 above that there
are no implied terms in a private contract for sale, as to quality and the fitness for purpose.
In the absence of any statements by the seller that would amount to conditions or
warranties, it is for the buyer to satisfy themselves as to those matters. Ms Gardiner, in
her evidence before me, accepted that Mr Smith had made no comment on any of the
issues contained in the D and B Auto Repair report before she purchased the vehicle. Ms
Gardiner was not, in my view, in a position where she could reject the vehicle and
repudiate the contract or claim damages; however, in attempting to do so, she left the
vehicle at Mr Smith’s premises, and he subsequently disposed of it. However, the matter
does not rest there either.
50. Ms Gardiner’s evidence was that following her leaving the vehicle in Mr Smith’s yard,
she heard nothing further from him despite her attempts to contact him. Mr Smith’s
evidence before me was that he did not contact Ms Gardiner after she had left the vehicle
and before he instructed his cousin to have the vehicle removed.
51. I am of the view that when the vehicle was left at Mr Smith’s property, he became an
involuntary or gratuitous bailee of it; alternatively, by disposing of it, Mr Smith has
converted the vehicle to his own use.
52. An involuntary bailment arises where a person is in possession of goods and aware of the
possession, but has given no consent to it.12
53. I refer to the passages from Halsbury’s Laws of England (2020), Volume 4, Bailment and
Pledge, where the learned authors state at paragraph 112:
“112. Deposit without agreement.
While the orthodox or literal transaction by way of depositum is a consensual
12 See the Encyclopaedia of Form and Precedents – LexisNexis at BAILMENT vol 3(1) > Commentary > F:
INVOLUNTARY BAILMENT AND BAILMENT BY CONCEALMENT 1: at para. 45.
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 12 of 16

bailment (deriving from express agreement between bailor and bailee) there
exist certain analogous forms of relationship based on one person's unrewarded
possession of another's goods which, while not directly the product of
agreement, may produce obligations akin to those of an orthodox bailment by
way of depositum and might in some cases have traditionally been viewed as
deriving from an implied agreement as to possession.”
54. And at paragraph 120:
“120. Involuntary deposit.
Where a chattel is sent, without request or arrangement, by one person to
another who does not hold himself out as willing to receive it, the person to
whom it is sent is deemed to be an involuntary bailee and will ordinarily owe no
responsibility to the sender to exercise reasonable care for its safe custody or
protection, but must not deliberately convert it to his own use and must take
reasonable care when purporting to redeliver the chattel to the owner or his
authorised representative (which would otherwise render him liable in
conversion). It has, however, been suggested that an involuntary bailee may owe
a duty of care, the standard varying according to what is reasonable in the
particular circumstances. In most cases, the standard would be low because of
the involuntary nature of the transaction, but in some a higher standard would
apply.”
55. What duty then did Mr Smith owe to Ms Gardiner? The duties of an involuntary bailee
are less than those of a voluntary bailee. The learned authors of the Encyclopaedia of
Forms and Precedents say:13
“The traditional view is that the involuntary bailee owes no duty to take
reasonable care to guard the goods against loss, theft or physical injury.
Provided the involuntary bailee does not attempt to deliver them to a third party,
they need only abstain from wilful or reckless conduct towards them.
…
13 Ibid at para. 46
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 13 of 16

It had been argued that the involuntary bailee’s duty should be one of reasonable
care in all the circumstances, but authority favouring this had been slight.
However, more recent authority is in support of this view— that its obligation in
law as involuntary bailee is to do what is right and reasonable in the
circumstances of the case.”
56. In Scotland -v- Solomon14 David Kitchin QC, sitting as a Deputy Judge of the High
Court15 stated:
“The obligations of an involuntary bailee were explained by Hawke J. in Elvin
and Powell Ltd. v Plummer Roddis Ltd. (1933) 50 TLR 158. If persons are
involuntary bailees and have done everything reasonable they are not liable to
pay damages if something which they do results in the loss of the property. There
is an obligation on the part of involuntary bailees to do what is right and
reasonable. (Emphasis added)
57. The application was an appeal from the Master who had refused summary judgment in
favour of the Solomons, or alternatively, to strike out the claim. The matter concerned the
sale of a property under a charging order. After the property was sold, the Solomons
obtained a possession order, which was executed by the bailiff. Belongings of the
Scotlands’ remained in the property and when the house was sold, the belongings were
either converted to the purchaser or they were disposed of by the purchaser. The Scotlands
brought a claim for the value of the items lost.
58. The court held that if the Scotlands’ case was proved, when the Solomons took possession
of the house, they became involuntary or gratuitous bailees of the possessions, and they
would have been under an obligation to do what was right and reasonable. The court noted
that the Solomons’ solicitor “knew Mr Scotland's new address from early July, so there
was no difficulty in communicating with him to tell him the house was being sold and that
he must remove his possessions if he wanted to”.
14 [2002] EWHC 1886.
15 The principle of law being cited with approval in Da Rocha‑Afodu and another -v- Mortgage Express Ltd and
another [2014] EWCA Civ 454 and Credit Agricole Corporate and Investment Bank -v- Persons with an Immediate
Right to Possess Goods and others [2025] EWHC 1346 (Ch).
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 14 of 16

59. The principle that an involuntary bailee has an obligation to do what is right and
reasonable was further expounded in Da Rocha‑Afodu and another -v- Mortgage
Express Ltd and another16 by Arden LJ, who held that, in considering whether the
involuntary bailee had done what was right and reasonable, the Court had to be alert to all
the particular circumstances in the case. That reasoning was later applied in Campbell -
v- Redstone Mortgages Limited.17
60. In my judgment, Mr Smith was obliged to act reasonably and to do what was right in
respect of how he dealt with the vehicle. He was aware that there was a dispute over the
sale and condition of the vehicle. Ms Gardiner was in contact with him, but he declined
to respond to her. He did not ask Ms Gardiner to remove the vehicle from his yard and did
not give her any notice that he intended to dispose of it.
61. Mr Smith did not act reasonably when he gave instructions to his cousin to have the
vehicle removed without first informing Ms Gardiner of his intentions. As a result, the
vehicle is now a total loss.
62. The question that arises is what, if anything, is Ms Gardiner entitled to recover? The
learned Magistrate found that there was a failure of consideration, in that Mr Smith failed
to transfer title in the vehicle to Ms Gardiner and awarded her the full $7,000.00.
63. For the reasons I have given, I do not agree that there was no transfer of title to Ms
Gardiner, so the Magistrate’s decision cannot stand. Even if the parties had attended at the
DMV and the vehicle had been registered to Ms Gardiner, the real dispute would have
still remained as the vehicle required work to be carried out on it.
64. I have also found on the principle of caveat emptor, that Ms Gardiner did not have a right
to reject the vehicle and demand repayment of the monies paid, however she is now in a
position where she is out of pocket and has lost the vehicle as a result of Mr Smith’s breach
of his obligation. She cannot now have the vehicle put into a roadworthy condition, and it
is on that premise that I approach an assessment of damages.
65. In Scotland, the court held:
16 Ibid.
17 [2014] EWHC 3081 (Ch).
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 15 of 16

“The Master had no doubt, and I agree, that the valuations put on many of the
items by Mr Scotland are grossly exaggerated. Nevertheless, I also agree with
the Master that it is impossible at this stage to say that the items left at the
premises had no value. There is a real issue as to what goods remained at the
premises after the visits in June and what their value was.
66. Unfortunately, the report from D and B Auto Repair, whilst listing the parts that needed
replacing, did not provide any costing for those parts nor any estimate of the costs of
carrying out the work.
67. The price Ms Gardiner paid for the vehicle is a useful starting point for assessing her loss.
In my view, however, the price Ms Gardiner paid does not reflect the value of the vehicle
at the time of the loss. Whilst I often refer to a definition of ‘value’ as ‘being the price a
willing buyer, acting without compulsion, is willing to pay at any given time’, that cannot
apply in this instance. Had Ms Gardiner had the foresight to have the vehicle inspected, it
seems to me that it is plainly evident that she would have negotiated a lower price (or
perhaps not purchased the vehicle at all, but then there would have been no issue with
which to trouble the Court).
68. In my judgment, the appropriate measure of damages is the value of the vehicle in its
unrepaired state, for it is that which has been lost. I was not addressed by either party on
the costs of the parts and work required. I would therefore invite them to make further
submissions on that issue and on the issue of costs.
Disposition
69. The order of the learned Magistrate dated 3 October 2023 is set aside.
70. Judgment is entered for the Plaintiff for damages to be assessed together with costs.
11th March 2026
The Hon. Justice Anthony S. Gruchot
Judge of the Supreme Court
CL APM 03/23 – Alvirto Ali Alexander Smith -v- Shalia Gardiner
Page 16 of 16
court: Supreme Court of Turks and Caicos Islands
summary: Seller converted a sold vehicle by disposing of it, giving the buyer a claim for damages despite title transfer.
